elements.sent-2411-001: Computing a Euclidean minimum spanning tree of a set of points is a seminal problem in computational geometry and geometric graph theory.
elements.sent-2411-002: We combine it with another classical problem in graph drawing, namely computing a monotone geometric representation of a given graph.
elements.sent-2411-003: More formally, given a finite set~$S$ of points in the plane and a finite set~$\cal D$ of directions, a geometric spanning tree~$T$ with vertex set~$S$ is ${\cal D$-monotone if, for every pair $\{u,v\}$ of vertices of~$T$, there exists a direction $d \in \cal D$ for which the unique path from~$u$ to~$v$ in~$T$ is monotone with respect to $d$.}
elements.sent-2411-004: We provide a characterization of ${\cal D}$-monotone spanning trees.
elements.sent-2411-005: Based on it, we show that a ${\cal D}$-monotone spanning tree of minimum length can be computed in polynomial time if the number $k=|{\cal D |$ of directions is fixed, both when (i)~the set ${\cal D}$ of directions is prescribed and when (ii)~the objective is to find a minimum-length ${\cal D}$-monotone spanning tree over all sets ${\cal D}$ of $k$ directions.}
elements.sent-2411-006: For $k = 2$, we describe algorithms that are much faster than those for the general case.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, in contrast to the classical Euclidean minimum spanning tree, whose vertex degree is at most six, we show that for every even integer~$k$, there exists a point set~$S_k$ and a set $\cal D$ of $k$ directions such that any minimum-length $\cal D$-monotone spanning tree of $S_k$ has maximum vertex degree~$2k$.
elements.sent-2411-008: We study a problem that combines the notion of minimum spanning tree of a set of points in the plane with the notion of monotone drawings of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-009: The problem of computing a (Euclidean) minimum spanning tree (MST) of a set of points in the plane is a well-established topic with a long history in computational geometry~\cite{GrahamH85 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: An MST of a finite set~$S$ of points is a geometric tree~$T$ such that: $(i)$~$T$ spans $S$, i.e., the vertices of $T$ are the points of $S$, and $(ii)$~$T$ has minimum length subject to property~$(i)$, where the length of $T$ is the sum of the lengths of its edges and the length of an edge is the Euclidean distance of its endpoints.
elements.sent-2411-011: Equivalently, the MST is the minimum spanning tree of the complete graph on $S$ where the weight of each edge is the Euclidean distance of its incident vertices.
elements.sent-2411-012: It is known that an MST is a subgraph of a Delaunay triangulation~\cite{shamosHoey75 (see \cref{fi:monotone-example1,fi:monotone-example2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Given that the Delaunay triangulation has at most $3n-6$ edges, an MST of $n$ points can be computed in $O(n\log n)$ time in the real RAM model of computation by the algorithms of Kruskal~\cite{kruskal1956shortest or Jarn\'ik--Prim~\cite{Jarnik1930,Prim1957 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: For more background on MSTs, see a survey by Eppstein~\cite{eppstein2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The problem has also been studied in higher dimensions~\cite{agarwalESW91,NarasimhanZZ2000,yao82 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Monotone drawings of graphs have been introduced by the authors of~\cite{AngeliniCBFP12 and have received considerable attention in recent years.}
elements.sent-2411-017: They are related to other types of drawings of graphs, such as angle-monotone~\cite{bakhshesh21,bakhshesh22,bonichon16,dehkordi14,lubiw18 , upward~\cite{dett-gd-99,DBLP:reference/algo/Didimo16,gt-upt-95 , greedy~\cite{DBLP:journals/tcs/AngeliniBDGKMPS19,angelini10,dehkordi14,Dhandapani2010,Papadimitriou2005,Rao2003 , self-approaching~\cite{alamdari13,bakhshesh19,nollenburg16 , and increasing-chord drawings~\cite{bahoo17,dehkordi14,mastakas15,nollenburg16 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Computing monotone drawings is also related to the geometric problem of finding monotone trajectories between two given points in the plane avoiding convex obstacles~\cite{DBLP:conf/compgeom/ArkinCM89 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Informally speaking, a plane path is monotone with respect to a direction~$d$ if the order of its vertices along the path coincides with the order of their projections on a line parallel to~$d$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Any monotone path is necessarily crossing-free~\cite{AngeliniCBFP12 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: A straight-line drawing of a graph~$G$ in the plane is monotone if there exists a monotone path (with respect to some direction) between any two vertices of~$G$; the direction of monotonicity may be different for each path.
elements.sent-2411-022: If the directions of monotonicity for the paths are restricted to a set \D of directions, then the drawing is \D-monotone.
elements.sent-2411-023: Results about monotone drawings include algorithms for different graph classes \cite{angelini17,AngeliniCBFP12,angeliniDKMRSW15,felsnerIKKMS16 and the study of the area requirement of such drawings (see \cite{hehe16,hehe17,hehe15,kindermannSSW14,oikonomouSym18 for monotone drawings of trees and \cite{hehe15b,hossainR15,oikonomouSymv17 for different classes of planar graphs).}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{minipage}{.71\textwidth} \begin{subfigure}{.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[page=1,width=\textwidth]{monotone_tree_example} \subcaption{} \label{fi:monotone-example1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[page=2,width=\textwidth]{monotone_tree_example} \subcaption{} \label{fi:monotone-example2} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[page=3,width=\textwidth]{monotone_tree_example} \subcaption{} \label{fi:monotone-example3} \end{subfigure} \caption{(a) A point set $S$ with its Delaunay triangulation, (b)~MST of~$S$, (c)~$\MMST$ of~$S$ w.r.t.\ $\{\binom{1}{0},\binom{0}{1}\}$. The $v_2$--$v_3$ path is $x$-monotone; the $v_1$--$v_2$ and $v_1$--$v_3$ paths are $y$-monotone.} \label{fi:monotone-example} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{.26\textwidth} \includegraphics[page=1,width=\textwidth]{wedges} \caption{ The set $\mathcal{W}_\D(p)$ for the point $p$ and the set $\D=\{d_1,d_2,d_3\}$.} \label{fi:wedges} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-024: In this paper, we study a natural setting that combines the benefits of spanning trees of minimum length with the benefits of monotone drawings.
elements.sent-2411-025: Namely, given a set $S$ of $n$ points in the plane and a prescribed set~\D of directions, we study the problem $\MMST(S,\D)$ of computing a \D-monotone spanning tree of~$S$ of minimum length (see \cref{fi:monotone-example3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: We call such a tree a minimum $\D$-monotone spanning tree.
elements.sent-2411-027: For a point set $S$ and an integer $k \geq 1$, we also address the problem $\MMST(S,k)$ of computing a minimum $k$-directional monotone spanning tree of~$S$, i.e., a \D-monotone spanning tree of minimum length among all possible sets~\D of $k$ directions.
elements.sent-2411-028: In this variant, the choice of the directions of monotonicity adjusts to the given point set, which can lead to shorter monotone spanning trees.
elements.sent-2411-029: We remark that there are other prominent attempts in the literature to couple the MST problem with an additional property.
elements.sent-2411-030: For example, the Euclidean degree-$\Delta$ MST asks for an MST whose maximum degree is bounded by a given integer $\Delta$~\cite{franckeHoffmann09,PapadimitriouVazirani84 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Seo, Lee, and Lin~\cite{SeoLL09 studied MSTs of smallest diameter or smallest radius.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Finding the $k$ smallest spanning trees \cite{k-mst-eppstein,k-mst-Frederickson,k-mst-gabow or dynamic MSTs \cite{dmst-chin,dmst-spira are further problems related to spanning trees.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Particularly relevant to our study is the rooted monotone MST problem introduced by Mastakas and Symvonis~\cite{mastakasSymv17 and further studied by Mastakas~\cite{mastakas21 .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In that problem, given a set $S$ of $n$ points with a designated root $r \in S$, the task is to compute an MST such that the path from $r$ to any other point of~$S$ is monotone.
elements.sent-2411-035: Mastakas~\cite{mastakas18 extended this setting to multiple roots.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The main results in this paper are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-037: We provide a characterization of \D-monotone spanning trees; see \cref{se:char .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Based on it, we show how to solve $\MMST(S,\D)$ in $O(f(|\D|)n^{2|\D|-1}\log n)$ time for some function~$f$ of~$|\D|$; see \cref{se:k-monotone .}
elements.sent-2411-039: In other words, $\MMST(S,\D)$ is~in~XP~(that~is, slicewise polynomial) when parameterized by~$|\D|$.
elements.sent-2411-040: For $|\D|=2$, we show how to solve $\MMST(S,\D)$ in $O(n^2)$ time.
elements.sent-2411-041: Regarding $\MMST(S,k)$, we describe $O(n^2\log n)$- and $O(n^6)$-time algorithms for $k=1$ and $k=2$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-042: For $k \geq 3$, we present an XP-algorithm that runs in $O(f(k)n^{2k(2k-1)}\log n)$ time; see \cref{se:k-monotone\arxivlncs{,se:appendix-mmst-s-k { .}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: We show that, in contrast to the MST, whose vertex degree is at most six~\cite{PapadimitriouVazirani84 , for every even integer~$k \geq 2$, there exists a point set~$S_k$ and a set \D of $k$ directions such that any minimum-length \D-monotone spanning tree of $S_k$ has maximum vertex degree~$2k$; see \cref{se:maxdeg .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Let $C$ denote the unit circle centered at the origin~$o$ of~$\mathbb{R ^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Any segment oriented from the center of $C$ to a point of $C$ defines a direction vector or simply a direction.
elements.sent-2411-046: Two directions are opposite if the two segments that define them belong to the same line and lie on opposite sides of the origin.
elements.sent-2411-047: Given a direction~$d$ and a set~$S$ of points in the plane, we say that $S$ is in $d$-general position if no two points in~$S$ lie on a line orthogonal to~$d$.
elements.sent-2411-048: If $S$ is in $d$-general position, let $\ord(S,d)$ be the linear ordering of the orthogonal projections of the points of~$S$ on any line parallel to~$d$ and directed as $d$; note that $\ord(S,d)$ is uniquely defined.
elements.sent-2411-049: Given a direction~$d$ and a point set $S=\{p_1,\dots,p_n \}$ in $d$-general position, we say that the geometric path $\langle p_1,\dots,p_n \rangle$ is $d$-monotone if $\ord(S,d)=\langle p_1,\dots,p_n \rangle$ or $\ord(S,d)=\langle p_n,\dots,p_1 \rangle$; in this case, all projections of the oriented segments $\overrightarrow{p_i p_{i+1 }$ (for $i \in \{1,\dots,n-1\})$ on a line parallel to $d$ point towards the same direction.}
elements.sent-2411-050: A path is monotone if it is $d$-monotone with respect to some direction~$d$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Let $S$ be a finite set of points, and let~\D be a finite set of directions such that no two of them are opposite.
elements.sent-2411-052: A spanning tree $T$ of $S$ is \D-monotone if, for every pair of vertices $\{u,v\}$ of~$T$, there is a $d \in \D$ such that the unique geometric path from~$u$ to~$v$ in~$T$ is $d$-monotone (which requires that the subset of points on the path from $u$ to $v$ is in $d$-general~position).
elements.sent-2411-053: A minimum \D-monotone spanning tree of~$S$ is a \D-monotone spanning tree of~$S$ of minimum length among all \D-monotone spanning trees of~$S$; we call $\MMST(S,\D)$ the problem of computing such a tree.
elements.sent-2411-054: For a positive integer~$k$, we say that a spanning tree~$T$ of~$S$ is $k$-directional monotone if there exists a set~\D of~$k$ directions such that $T$ is \D-monotone.
elements.sent-2411-055: A minimum $k$-directional monotone spanning tree of~$S$ is a $k$-directional monotone spanning tree of~$S$ of minimum length among all $k$-directional monotone spanning trees of~$S$; we call $\MMST(S,k)$ the problem of computing such a tree.
elements.sent-2411-056: To solve this problem, it turns out that it is sufficient to consider only sets \D of directions such that $S$ is in \D-general position, i.e., $S$ is in $d$-general position for every $d \in \D$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Given two points $u$ and $v$, let~$l_{u,v$} be the line passing through~$u$ and~$v$.
elements.sent-2411-058: Given a direction $d$ and a point $x$, let $d(x)$ be the line parallel to~$d$ passing through $x$ and let $\overline{d$} be the direction orthogonal to~$d$ obtained by rotating~$d$ counterclockwise (\ccw) by an angle of~$90^\circ$.
elements.sent-2411-059: Accordingly, $\overline{d(x)$} is the line orthogonal to~$d(x)$ and $\overline{l_{u,v (x)$} is the line orthogonal to~$l_{u,v}$ passing through~$x$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Given two vertices~$u$ and~$v$ of a geometric tree~$T$, let $\Path{u{v}$} denote the path of $T$~from~$u$~to~$v$.
elements.sent-2411-061: Given a sorted set $\D=\{d_1,d_2, \ldots, d_k\}$ of $k \ge 1$ pairwise non-opposite directions (assumed to be sorted with respect to the directions' slopes) and a point $p$ in the plane, let $\mathcal{W_\D(p)$} $=\{W_0(p), W_1(p), \ldots, W_{2k-1 (p)\}$ be the set of $2k$ wedges determined by the lines $\overline{d_1 (p), \overline{d_2 (p), \dots, \overline{d_k (p)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: See \cref{fi:wedges on page~\pageref{fi:wedges for an illustration where $k=3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: We fix the numbering of the wedges by starting with an arbitrary wedge~$W_0(p)$ and then continue with $W_1(p), W_2(p), \dots, W_{2k-1 (p)$ in \ccw order around~$p$.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Whenever we refer to a wedge $W_i(p)$ for some integer~$i$, we assume that~$i$ is taken modulo~$2k$.
elements.sent-2411-065: If $p$ coincides with the origin~$o$, we just write $\mathcal{W_\D$} $=\{W_0, W_1, \ldots, W_{2k-1}\}$ instead of $\mathcal{W _\D(o)=\{W_0(o), W_1(o), \ldots, W_{2k-1 (o)\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: For a directed geometric path $P=\langle p_1,\dots,p_r \rangle$ and $i \in [r-1]$, let~$c_i(P)$ be the oriented segment starting from the origin~$o$ that is parallel to and has the same orientation as $\overrightarrow{p_i p_{i+1 }$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Define $\sec(P)$, the sector of directions of path~$P$, to be the smallest sector of the unit circle that includes the oriented segment $c_i(P)$ for every $i\in[r-1]$; see \cref{fig:path_1,fig:path_2 .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{.3\linewidth} \includegraphics[page=1]{path} \subcaption{} \label{fig:path_1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.3\linewidth} \includegraphics[page=2]{path} \subcaption{} \label{fig:path_2} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.3\linewidth} \includegraphics[page=3]{path} \subcaption{} \label{fig:path_3} \end{subfigure} \caption{(a) A directed geometric path $P$, (b) its sector of directions $\sec(P)$ (in dark gray) and (c) the wedge set $\Wutil_P$ of path $P$ (in blue).}
elements.sent-2411-068: Moreover, let $\Wutil_{P$} $\subseteq \mathcal{W _\D$ be the wedge set of the directed path~$P$, i.e., the smallest set of consecutive wedges in counterclockwise (\ccw) order whose union contains~$\sec(P)$; see \cref{fig:path_3 .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: For a point~$p$, let~$\Wutil_P(p)$ be the region of the plane determined by $\Wutil_P$ translated such that $p$ is its apex.
elements.sent-2411-070: If~$\overleftarrow{P}$ is the reverse path of~$P$, then $\Wutil_{\overleftarrow{P }$ consists of the wedges opposite to those in~$\Wutil_{P}$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: We say that path~$P$ utilizes wedge set $\Wutil_{P}$.
elements.sent-2411-072: In a \D-monotone spanning tree~$T$, a branching vertex is a vertex of degree at least~3 and a leaf path is a path of degree-2 vertices from a branching vertex to a leaf.
elements.sent-2411-073: Given two adjacent branching vertices~$u$ and~$v$ in~$T$, the branch $\Branch{u{v}$} is the unique path that connects~$u$ and~$v$ via a sequence of degree-2 vertices.
elements.sent-2411-074: Both a leaf path and a branch may consist of a single edge.
elements.sent-2411-075: Further, for any pair of (not necessarily adjacent) vertices~$u$ and~$v$, let~$\Subtree{u{v}$} be the subtree of~$T$ consisting of~$u$ and all subtrees hanging from~$u$ except for the one containing~$v$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Let $\WS{u{v}$} $\subseteq \W_\D$, the wedge set of~$\Subtree{u {v}$, be the smallest set of consecutive wedges that contains all wedges utilized by either leaf paths or branches oriented away from~$u$ in~$\Subtree{u {v}$ and that does not contain the wedge utilized by the edge out of~$u$ that leads to vertex~$v$; see \cref{fig:Wuv_wedgeSet .}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Note that if~$u$ and/or $v$ is a leaf, then $\WS{u {v = \emptyset$ and/or $\WS{v {u = \emptyset$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Let $\WS{u{v (u)$} be the region defined by the wedges in $\WS{u {v}$ translated such that~$u$ is their apex.}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{subfigure}{.54\textwidth} \includegraphics[page=1]{wedges_util} \subcaption{} \label{fig:Wuv_wedgeSet1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.46\textwidth} \includegraphics[page=2]{wedges_util} \subcaption{} \label{fig:Wuv_wedgeSet2} \end{subfigure} \caption{(a) A monotone tree and its sets of utilized wedges for each leaf path. (b)~All sets of utilized wedges drawn on the same unit circle. Set $\WS{u}{v}$ (resp. $\WS{v}{u}$) consists of all wedges in the blue (resp. gray)~region.} \label{fig:Wuv_wedgeSet}
elements.section-2411-003: Properties of Monotone Paths and Trees
elements.sent-2411-079: We describe basic properties of monotone paths and \D-monotone trees, which we use in \cref{se:char .}
elements.sent-2411-080: Unless otherwise stated, we assume that \D consists of pairwise non-opposite directions and that the point set $S$ is always in \D-general position.

elements.sent-2411-082: If $u$ and $v$ lie in the same half-plane determined by $\overline{d (x)$, then the path $P$ is not $d$-monotone.}

elements.sent-2411-084: Let $x$, $u$, and $v$ be points in~$S$ such that $x \in \Path{u {v}$.}
elements.sent-2411-085: If $u$ and $v$ lie in the same wedge in $\W_\D(x)$, then the path~$\Path{u {v}$ is not \D-monotone.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Let $\Delta(T)$ denote the maximum degree of tree~$T$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Then, $P$ is monotone if and only if the angle of its sector of directions $\sec(P)$ is smaller than $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-089: While \cref{lemma:Angelini can be used to recognize monotone paths, it does not specify a direction of monotonicity.}

elements.sent-2411-093: Given a direction $d \in \D$, $P$ is $d$-monotone if and only if $\overline{d (o)$ does not intersect the interior of $\Wutil_P$, where $o$ is the origin.}

elements.sent-2411-147: In this section we prove that the problem $\MMST(S,\D)$ is in XP with respect to~$|\D|$, that is, it can be solved in polynomial time for any fixed value of~$|\D|$.
elements.sent-2411-148: An embedding of a tree is prescribed by the clockwise circular order of the edges incident to each vertex of the tree.
elements.sent-2411-149: A tree with a given embedding is an embedded tree.
elements.sent-2411-150: A homeomorphically irreducible tree (HIT), is an embedded tree without vertices of degree two~\cite{10.1007/BF02559543 .}
elements.sent-2411-151: Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ be two trees; we say that $T_1$ and $T_2$ have the same topology if they are (possibly different) subdivisions of the same HIT $H$.
elements.sent-2411-152: Two trees with the same topology have the same embedding if the circular order of the edges around the vertices is the same in both trees.
elements.sent-2411-153: Given a HIT~$H$ and any embedded tree~$T$ that is a subdivision of~$H$, we say that $H$ corresponds to~$T$.
elements.sent-2411-154: Since for a vertex of degree two the circular order of its incident edges is unique, the embedding of a tree $T$ uniquely defines the embedding of the corresponding HIT.
elements.sent-2411-155: Note that, given an embedded tree $T$ and the corresponding HIT $H$, an internal vertex of~$H$ corresponds to a branching vertex of~$T$, a leaf of~$H$ to a leaf path of $T$, and an edge between two internal vertices of~$H$ to a branch of~$T$.
elements.sent-2411-156: Let $n_{\ell}$ be the numbers of HITs with at most $\ell$ leaves.
elements.sent-2411-157: We can use a result of Harary, Robinson, and Schwenk~\cite{harary_robinson_schwenk_1975 concerning the number of (non-embedded) trees with $2\ell-2$ vertices to derive a bound for~$n_\ell$.}
elements.sent-2411-158: However, this does not yield an algorithm to generate all different HITs with at most $\ell$ leaves.
elements.sent-2411-159: For this reason we give an upper bound that is based on a generation scheme.
elements.sent-2411-160: Note that our scheme may generate the same HIT several times.
elements.sent-2411-161: It examines every HIT with at most $2k$ leaves.
elements.sent-2411-162: Since there are many (\D-monotone) spanning trees that are subdivisions of the same HIT, the algorithm examines for each HIT all of its \D-monotone spanning trees on $S$.
elements.sent-2411-163: Let~$H$ be the HIT under consideration, and let $\ell$ and $b$ be the numbers of leaves and branching vertices of $H$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-164: Let $M$ be one of the $O(n^b)$ possible mappings of the $b$ branching vertices to points in~$S$.
elements.sent-2411-165: Let $A$ be an assignment of the wedges of $\W_\D$ to the leaves of $H$ so that each leaf receives a distinct set of consecutive wedges.
elements.sent-2411-166: Assigning (as part of~$A$) the set of consecutive wedges $\W^A$ to a leaf $\lambda$ incident to a branching vertex $v$ of $H$ can be interpreted as our intention to cover all points in region $\W^A(v)$ by the monotone leaf path~$P$ that ends at~$\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-167: As shown in \cref{fig:assigned_used_wedges , the monotone leaf path may utilize a set of consecutive wedges $\Wutil_{P \subseteq \W^A$, i.e., some of the leading and/or trailing wedges of $\W^A$ may not be utilized by~$P$.}}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[page=1]{assigned_used_wedges} \caption{A leaf path $P$ that is assigned seven wedges but utilizes only five of them (shaded darkgray): It is not monotone with respect to $\{d_3, d_4, d_5, d_6\}$.} \label{fig:assigned_used_wedges}
elements.sent-2411-168: The point set~$S$, the set \D of $k$ (pairwise non-opposite) directions, the HIT~$H$, together with mapping $M$ and assignment~$A$, form an instance of a restricted problem that asks for a minimum \D-monotone spanning tree having $H$ as its HIT and respecting~$M$ and~$A$.
elements.sent-2411-169: Let $\MMST(S, \D, H, M, A)$ denote this problem instance.
elements.sent-2411-170: Note that such a monotone spanning tree may not exist.
elements.sent-2411-171: If it exists, it turns out that it is unique (see \cref{le:mmst_sdtma ).}
elements.sent-2411-172: The algorithm for solving instances of type $\MMST(S, \D, H, M, A)$ is repeatedly used by the algorithm that proves \cref{thm:general-k .}
elements.sent-2411-173: Then, $\MMST(S, \D, H, M, A)$ can be solved in~$O(n\log n+nk+k)$~time.
elements.sent-2411-174: Moreover, if a solution to the $\MMST(S, \D, H, M, A)$ exists, then it is unique.
elements.sent-2411-175: Condition~(b) of \cref{thm:Branches_large_Wuv is satisfied by definition since $A$ is a valid assignment.}
elements.sent-2411-176: For condition (c), we first compute the set $\mathcal{R}$ that consists of all path regions and branch regions and for every branch $\Branch{u {v}$, we compute $\WS{u {v}$ and $\WS{v {u}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: These computations take $O(k)$ time since HIT $H$ has size $O(k)$.
elements.sent-2411-178: Then, the algorithm verifies, for every edge $(u,v)$ of $H$, whether regions $\WS{u {v (u)$ and $\Region{u {v}$ are disjoint, in $O(k)$ time.}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: For condition~(a), we compute, for every remaining point~$p$ in~$S$, the region of $\mathcal{R}$ that contains $p$, in $O(nk)$ time.
elements.sent-2411-180: We then check, for every region in~$\mathcal{R}$, whether there exists a path that (i)~is monotone with respect to the two directions that are orthogonal to its boundaries and (ii)~spans all points in the region.
elements.sent-2411-181: This can be done in $O(n\log n)$ time by sorting the points according to both directions.
elements.sent-2411-182: If the spanning tree exists, its uniqueness follows from the fact that each region in~$\mathcal{R}$ contains a unique \D-monotone path.
elements.sent-2411-183: There exists a function $f \colon \mathbb{N \to \mathbb{N}$ such that, if $S$ is in \D-general position, then we can compute a minimum \D-monotone spanning tree of~$S$ in $O(f(k) \cdot n^{2k-1 \log n)$ time.}}
elements.sent-2411-184: In other words, the problem $\MMST(S,\D)$ is in XP when parameterized by~$k$.
elements.sent-2411-185: Hence, a \D-monotone spanning tree has at most $2k$ leaves and at most $2k-2$ branching vertices.
elements.sent-2411-186: We enumerate the at most $7^{2k \cdot (2k)!$ HITs according to \cref{le:numEmbeddedTrees .}}
elements.sent-2411-187: Let~$H$ be the current HIT, let $\ell$ be the number of leaves, and let $b\le \ell-2$ be the number of branching vertices of $H$.
elements.sent-2411-188: We go through each of the $O(n^b)=O(n^{2k-2 )$ subsets of cardinality $b \le 2k-2$ of~$S$.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Let $M$ be the mapping of the branching vertices of~$H$ to points in~$S$.
elements.sent-2411-190: Let $A$ be the assignment of a set of consecutive wedges in $\W_\D$ to the leaves of~$H$.
elements.sent-2411-191: There are at most $2k \cdot {{2k-1 \choose {\ell-1 \le 2k \cdot 2^{2k}$ many such assignments since we have $2k$ choices for mapping the first leaf to some wedge, and then we select $\ell-1$ out of the $2k-1$ remaining wedges that we attribute to a different leaf than the preceding wedge (in circular order).}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: For each of the $n^{2k-2}\cdot f_0(k)$, (with $f_0(k)=7^{2k \cdot (2k)! \cdot 2k \cdot 4^k \in 2^{O(k \log k)}$) choices of a HIT~$H$, mapping $M$ and assignment $A$, we run the algorithm presented in the proof of \cref{le:mmst_sdtma for the $\MMST(S, \D, H, M, A)$, which terminates in $O(n\log n+nk+k)$ time.}}
elements.sent-2411-193: Finally, we return the shortest tree that we have found (if any).
elements.sent-2411-194: The total runtime is $O(f(k) \cdot n^{2k-1}\log n)$, where $f(k)=f_0(k)\cdot k \in 2^{O(k \log k)}$.

elements.section-2411-006: Maximum Degree of the Minimum $k$-Directional MST
elements.sent-2411-198: Since the (Euclidean) MST has maximum degree at most six~\cite{GEORGAKOPOULOS1987122 , it is natural to ask whether this upper bound carries over to minimum $k$-directional monotone spanning trees.}
elements.sent-2411-199: We prove that this is not the case by presenting a set \D of $k$ specific directions and a set $S_k$ of $2k+1$ points such that the unique monotone $k$-directional spanning tree of~$S_k$ has degree~$2k$.
elements.sent-2411-200: Let $k$ be an even positive integer, and let $\D = \{d_1,d_2,\dots,d_k\}$ be the set of $k$ distinct (pairwise non-opposite) directions (in \ccw order) such that $d_1$ is defined by the vector $(1,0)$ and, for $1\leq i<k$, $\angle d_i d_{i+1 =\frac{\pi {k}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-201: Since $k$ is even, it holds that $\mathcal{W _{\D = \mathcal{W _{\overline{\D }$ where $\overline{\D = \{ \overline{d_1 , \overline{d_2 , \ldots, \overline{d_k \}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: For simplicity, we consider $W_0$ to be the wedge defined by $d_1$ and $d_2$.
elements.sent-2411-203: We define $S_k=\{o\} \cup \{v_0,v_1, \dots, v_{2k-1}\}$ to be the set of $2k+1$ points, where $o$ is the origin and, for $i \in [k-1]$, $v_i$ is placed on the unit circle in the (\ccw) second angle-trisection of wedge $W_i$ of $\mathcal{W _{\D}$; see \cref{fig:pointset .}}
elements.sent-2411-204: By construction $S_k\setminus\{o\}$ is the vertex set of a regular $2k$-gon centered at~$o$ and the star with edges $ov_0,\dots,ov_{2k-1}$ is a valid monotone spanning tree for~$S_k$ of length~$2k$.
elements.sent-2411-205: Thus, any solution of $\MMST(S_k, \D)$ has length at most~$2k$.
elements.sent-2411-206: Let $T$ be a tree that spans $S_k$.
elements.sent-2411-207: We call polygon vertices the vertices of $T$ distinct from $o$.
elements.sent-2411-208: We refer to edges of $T$ connecting adjacent polygon vertices as external, to edges incident to $o$ as~rays and to all other edges as chords.
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{subfigure}{.35\linewidth} \includegraphics[page=1]{2k-degree-proof-new} \subcaption{} \label{fig:pointset} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.3\linewidth} \includegraphics[page=2]{2k-degree-proof-new} \subcaption{} \label{fig:theorem_proof.1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.3\linewidth} \includegraphics[page=3]{2k-degree-proof-new} \subcaption{} \label{fig:mmsg} \end{subfigure} \caption{(a) The point set $S_k$ is defined based on the set $\mathcal{W}_{\D}$ of wedges (red dashed). (b)~The path setting exploited in the proof of \cref{theorem:deg_star}. (c)~A monotone spanning graph of the point set in \cref{fig:pointset} whose length is much smaller than the $2k$-star in~(a).} \label{fig:pointset-full}
elements.sent-2411-209: To show that the unique solution to the instance $\MMST(S_k,\D)$ is the $2k$-star centered at~$o$, we first establish that polygon vertices have degree at most~2.
elements.sent-2411-211: It is easy to show that a leaf of $T$ cannot be the endpoint of a chord.
elements.sent-2411-212: By using this property together with the fact that all polygon vertices of $T$ have degree at most~2 (\cref{lemma:DegreeTwo ), we can show that $T$ must contain the path $P=\langle v_{2k-1 , v_0, v_{2k-2 , \dots, v_{2k-1-i , v_i, \dots, v_{\frac{k {2 -1 , o \rangle$, (\cref{fig:theorem_proof.1 ).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Consider the tree $T^\prime$ formed by replacing the edges of~$P$ by rays from~$o$ to the path vertices.
elements.sent-2411-215: To show that $T$ is not optimal, it suffices to show that the length $\|P\|$ of $P$ is greater than the total length of the rays that replaced the edges of $P$ in $T^\prime$ or, equivalently, that $\|P\| > k$.
elements.sent-2411-216: Indeed, using geometry, we show that $\|P\| = 1+\sum_{i=1 ^{k-1 2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi {2k i\right) = \cot\left(\frac{\pi {4k}\right) > k$.. \qed \end{sketchproof.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: We have presented an XP algorithm for solving $\MMST(S,k)$.
elements.sent-2411-218: It is natural to ask whether this problem is NP-hard if $k$ is part of the input (rather than a fixed constant).
elements.sent-2411-219: Another research direction is to study, for a given point set~$S$ and a set~\D of directions, the problem of computing a minimum \D-monotone spanning {\em graph for~$S$.}
elements.sent-2411-220: Note that such a graph can have much smaller total length than a solution to $\MMST(S,\D)$.
elements.sent-2411-221: Indeed, \cref{theorem:deg_star shows that there is a point set~$S_k$ (\cref{fig:pointset ) and a set \D of $k$ directions such that the only solution to $\MMST(S_k, \D)$ is the $2k$-star, which has a total length of $2k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-222: A monotone spanning {\em graph of~$S_k$ (see \cref{fig:mmsg ) has a total length of at most $2(\pi+1)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-223: Given a set $S$ of $n$ points, its Delaunay triangulation has at most $3n-6$ edges, hence an MST of~$S$ can be computed in $O(n\log n)$ time (in the real RAM model of computation) via standard MST algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-224: Eppstein~\cite{eppstein2000 has a survey on MSTs.}
elements.sent-2411-225: They are related to other types of drawings of graphs, such as angle-monotone~\cite{bakhshesh21,bakhshesh22,bonichon16,dehkordi14,lubiw18 , upward~\cite{DBLP:reference/algo/Didimo16,gt-upt-95 , greedy~\cite{DBLP:journals/tcs/AngeliniBDGKMPS19,angelini10,dehkordi14,Dhandapani2010,Papadimitriou2005,Rao2003 , self-approaching~\cite{alamdari13,bakhshesh19,nollenburg16 , and increasing-chord drawings~\cite{bahoo17,dehkordi14,mastakas15,nollenburg16 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-226: A plane path is monotone with respect to a direction~$d$ if the order of its vertices along the path coincides with the order of their projections on a line parallel to~$d$.
elements.sent-2411-227: Results about monotone drawings include algorithms for different graph classes \cite{angelini17,AngeliniCBFP12,angeliniDKMRSW15,felsnerIKKMS16 and the study of the area requirement of such drawings (see \cite{hehe17,kindermannSSW14,oikonomouSym18 for monotone drawings of trees and \cite{hehe15b,hossainR15,oikonomouSymv17 for different classes of planar graphs).}}}
